---
title: "Research"
pagetitle: "Research — Hugo Trad"
description: "Three threads: cognitive bias, the psychology of administrative burden, and audit design."
lang: en
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="https://hdtrad.github.io/en/research">
          <link rel="alternate" hreflang="fr" href="https://hdtrad.github.io/fr/recherche">
          <link rel="alternate" hreflang="ar" href="https://hdtrad.github.io/ar/research">
---

# Research {.hero__name}

My work sits at the intersection of cognitive psychology, behavioural science, and public administration. I ask why administrative systems accumulate complexity that nobody designed, and what cognitive mechanisms make that complexity sticky once it is in place.

Three threads run through the research:

(i) the **cognitive biases** that lead designers to add rather than remove — additive bias, outcome bias, moral framing;

(ii) the **psychology of administrative burden** — how citizens and employees experience friction;

(iii) the **design of audit methodologies and tools** that translate this evidence into actionable reform.

I collaborate occasionally with the OECD on sludge methodology and with national agencies on applied audits.

## Working papers

```{=html}
<article class="entry">
  <h3 class="entry__title">Additive bias in administrative design <span class="badge-status">in prep</span></h3>
  <p class="entry__meta">with Brent Strickland</p>
  <p class="entry__abstract">Why do designers of forms, procedures, and rules systematically add requirements rather than remove them, even when removal improves outcomes? Three experiments test additive bias as a cognitive driver of administrative accretion.</p>
</article>

<article class="entry">
  <h3 class="entry__title">Outcome bias and the persistence of sludge <span class="badge-status">in prep</span></h3>
  <p class="entry__abstract">When citizens are denied benefits because of administrative friction, observers blame the citizen, not the system. Five experiments link outcome-based evaluation to the persistence of sludge — and propose a process-based correction.</p>
</article>

<article class="entry">
  <h3 class="entry__title">Moral foundations of sludge tolerance <span class="badge-status">in prep</span></h3>
  <p class="entry__meta">with Brent Strickland</p>
  <p class="entry__abstract">Tolerance for administrative burden tracks moral framing: liberty-coded burdens are read as cost, fairness-coded burdens as protection. Two experiments map who tolerates which form of friction, and why.</p>
</article>
```

## Doctoral thesis

```{=html}
<article class="entry">
  <h3 class="entry__title">The War on Sludge: Uncovering the Cognitive Roots of Administrative Complexity <span class="badge-status badge-status--soft">PhD, 2024</span></h3>
  <p class="entry__meta">École normale supérieure, Institut Jean Nicod · Defended December 2024</p>
  <p class="entry__abstract">A cognitive-science framework for sludge: why complexity accumulates, why it is hard to remove, and what evidence-based reform requires. Sets out the audit methodology developed since.</p>
</article>
```
